generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance InjectionMoldingToolSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor component capturing real-time operational parameters of injection molding tools including temperature, pressure, and flow rates for process monitoring and control."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InjectionMoldingToolDataTransmitted
]

instance RoboticSystemTelemetrySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor system collecting telemetry data from robotic systems including position, velocity, acceleration, and force metrics for advanced robotics applications."
    DTDFVocab:producedData RoboticSystemDataTransmitted
]

instance EnvironmentalConditionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor measuring environmental parameters such as humidity, temperature, and vibration levels affecting manufacturing processes."
    DTDFVocab:producedData EnvironmentalConditionDataTransmitted
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance InjectionMoldingToolDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InjectionMoldingToolSensor
]

instance RoboticSystemDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom RoboticSystemTelemetrySensor
]

instance EnvironmentalConditionDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnvironmentalConditionSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance PhysicsBasedInjectionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "White-box physics-based model implemented in MathWorks Simulink representing dynamic behavior of injection molding processes including nonlinear friction and thermal dynamics."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkSimulationEnabler
]

instance DataDrivenFrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Black-box machine learning model capturing complex, nonlinear friction relationships in manufacturing processes using probabilistic latent variable techniques."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MachineLearningEnabler
]

instance SysMLInjectionArchitecture : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML-based component-connector architecture model defining functional decomposition of injection molding systems including interfaces and interactions."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler
]

instance StateMachineRobotControl : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model using SysML v1.7 state machines to describe logical/functional dynamics of robotic systems for control and monitoring purposes."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler
]

instance InjectionMoldingProcessData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from injection molding tools including raw sensor readings, processed KPIs, and purpose-driven abstractions for performance analysis."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InjectionMoldingToolDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance RoboticPerformanceData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed telemetry data from robotic systems including derived metrics for efficiency, accuracy, and predictive maintenance indicators."
    DTDFVocab:fromData RoboticSystemDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance EnvironmentalContextData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Contextual environmental data including filtered and abstracted readings relevant to manufacturing process conditions and quality control."
    DTDFVocab:fromData EnvironmentalConditionDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SimulinkSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink simulation engine enabling detailed behavioral modeling and dynamic system simulation for physics-based process validation."

]

instance MachineLearningEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "AI/ML framework enabling predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and optimization through integration of machine learning models into Digital Twin processes."

]

instance SysMLModelingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML-based modeling tool enabling functional/logical architecture modeling including components, connectors, and state machines for system decomposition."
    DTDFVocab:enables ArchitectureModelingService
]

instance DataAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Digital Shadow framework enabling collection, abstraction, and contextualization of raw sensor data into meaningful process insights."

]

instance MiddlewareIntegrationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [

]

// C6: Services

]

]

]

]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingDigitalTwinEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Digital environment hosting all components, models, and services for Industry 4.0 manufacturing systems including injection molding and robotic applications."
    base:contains InjectionMoldingToolSensor,
                 RoboticSystemTelemetrySensor,
                 EnvironmentalConditionSensor,
                 InjectionMoldingDigitalShadow,
                 RoboticDigitalShadow,
                 EnvironmentalContextComponent,
                 ProcessingComponentInjection,
                 ProcessingComponentRobotics,
                 TraceabilityFramework,
                 PhysicsBasedInjectionModel,
                 DataDrivenFrictionModel,
                 SysMLInjectionArchitecture,
                 StateMachineRobotControl,
                 SimulinkSimulationEnabler,
                 MachineLearningEnabler,
                 SysMLModelingEnabler,
                 DataAggregationEnabler,
                 MiddlewareIntegrationEnabler
]

// C2: Components
instance InjectionMoldingDigitalShadow : DTDFVocab:Component [

]

instance RoboticDigitalShadow : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Passive data collection component implementing the Digital Shadow Reference Model for one-way data flow from robotic systems to digital representations."

]

]

instance ProcessingComponentRobotics : DTDFVocab:Component [

]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is **not explicitly detailed** in the provided documents for Digital Twin systems. However, the context implies that while **Digital Shadows** are unidirectional (physical → digital) [32], a **Digital Twin (DT)** can include **active components** to push data back to the physical system [35–37]. Key inferred aspects (if applicable) would include:

1. **Control Flows**:
   - **Command types**: Likely **corrective actions** (e.g., parameter adjustments, maintenance alerts, or operational directives) derived from digital analysis.
   - **Message formats**: Standardized formats (e.g., JSON, XML, or protocol-specific payloads) for interoperability with physical systems.

2. **Validation and Authentication**:
   - **Security protocols** (e.g., TLS, OAuth) may be implied for command transmission, though not specified.
   - **Conflict resolution**: Not mentioned; potential mechanisms could include **priority-based routing** or **human-in-the-loop validation** for critical commands.

3. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Closed-loop feedback** is not described, but **event-driven responses** (e.g., triggering physical adjustments based on digital predictions) are plausible in advanced DT systems.

4. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - Not documented, but **acknowledgment mechanisms** (e.g., status updates from physical systems) would be critical for verification.

**Note**: The documents primarily focus on **Digital Shadows** (unidirectional) and do not provide technical specifics for **virtual-to-physical control flows** in Digital Twins. Thus, this characteristic is **Not in Document** for explicit details.
"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's temporal aspects are governed by the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**, which emphasizes **one-way automated data flow** between physical and digital components, requiring **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** for data trace aggregation and abstraction. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., millisecond, second-level granularity) are not detailed, the model mandates **fine-grained traceability** of data traces to their originating processing components, implying **low-latency requirements** for computational steps (e.g., simulation or data calculation models) to maintain consistency. The **Processing component** in the Digital Shadow Reference Model consumes input data traces and produces output traces, necessitating **temporal alignment** between physical system observations and digital model updates. No specific time scales (e.g., Hz, ms) or latency thresholds (e.g., <100ms) are quantified in the provided documents, but the **transitive traceability** of data traces suggests a need for **high-frequency data ingestion** to support dynamic system behavior modeling. The integration of **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) and **general-purpose languages** (e.g., C++, Java) further implies that the Digital Twin system must accommodate **variable time scales** depending on the computational demands of the underlying models (e.g., discrete-event simulation vs. continuous-time dynamics)."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin constellation** is orchestrated through a **reference model-based approach**, where the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** serves as a foundational framework for defining and integrating Digital Twin components. This model incorporates **architecture patterns** derived from **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**, including **component & connector perspectives** (e.g., SysML IBDs, AADL, or MontiArc) to describe the **functional and logical architecture** of the Digital Twin system. The **Processing component** acts as a **central computational hub**, consuming input data traces (e.g., sensor data, simulation outputs) and producing output traces via **data calculation or simulation models** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, C++/Java-based algorithms). The **concretization of the reference model** enables **customization of the Digital Twin constellation**, allowing for **modular integration of heterogeneous tools** (e.g., simulation engines, general-purpose programming languages) while maintaining **traceability** through associations between data traces, processing components, and models. Resource management is implicitly handled via the **purpose-driven design**, where the Digital Twin system allocates computational resources based on the **specific requirements** of the Digital Shadow (e.g., real-time vs. batch processing). System-wide coordination is achieved through **traceability links**, ensuring that the **transitive relationships** between data traces, processing components, and models remain intact across the constellation. No explicit mention of **orchestration frameworks** (e.g., Kubernetes, Docker) or **communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) is provided, but the model suggests a **service-oriented architecture** where components interact via **data-driven workflows**."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by the provided documents, is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which decompose system modeling into hierarchical abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. These layers are formalized using **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**, **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**. The lifecycle transitions between these stages involve **conforming models** derived from **reference models** (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model), where concrete realizations (e.g., UML Class Diagrams) incarnate abstract concepts via **conformance relations** (e.g., mapping incarnations to reference classes). The lifecycle governance is implicitly supported by **traceability mechanisms**—data traces originate from processing components (e.g., simulation or calculation models in MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) and maintain transitive traceability through associations. Metadata plays a critical role in facilitating data processing and abstraction across stages, ensuring alignment with domain-specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing use cases)."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system’s fidelity and validity rely on rigorous **modeling and data integrity frameworks**:
- **Model fidelity requirements** are met through:
  - **Physics-based validation** (e.g., Simulink simulations for robot dynamics) to ensure analytical correctness.
  - **Data-driven calibration** using scalable identification methods (e.g., Sorrentino et al.’s intrinsic model-based approaches) to adapt to nonlinear phenomena like friction.
  - **Hybrid validation** combining white-box interpretability with black-box accuracy (e.g., probabilistic latent variable models).

- **Validation and verification (V&V) methods** include:
  - **Traceability mechanisms** for transformations (e.g., SysML-to-simulation mappings) to ensure consistency across modeling tools.
  - **Cross-validation** between physical experiments and digital shadow data traces for empirical grounding.
  - **Uncertainty quantification** via probabilistic modeling to assess model confidence intervals.

- **Uncertainty sources** are addressed through:
  - **Sensor noise and sampling artifacts** (implicit in real-time data streams).
  - **Model approximation errors** (e.g., linearization assumptions in physics-based models).
  - **Data sparsity** in black-box models, mitigated via intrinsic calibration techniques.

- **Error detection and correction mechanisms** involve:
  - **Anomaly detection** in digital shadow data traces (e.g., outliers in friction coefficients).
  - **Adaptive recalibration** of models using online learning (e.g., updating ML parameters via intrinsic robot state data).
  - **Fallback mechanisms** to white-box models when black-box confidence drops below thresholds.

- **Quality assurance processes** include:
  - **Model-based development (MBSE) compliance** with SysML/AADL standards for structural integrity.
  - **Continuous integration** of simulation and real-world data for dynamic recalibration.
  - **Stakeholder-driven validation** to align digital representations with industrial requirements (e.g., robot friction modeling for manufacturing).
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **technical network infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems, as inferred from the documents, includes:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **MQTT** (as referenced in [52]) for lightweight, publish-subscribe IoT communication, enabling efficient data transmission from physical sensors to digital models.
   - **Open-source IoT platforms** like **ThingsBoard (v4.0.0)** [53] may use **RESTful APIs** or **MQTT brokers** for data ingestion and distribution.

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Distributed architectures** are implied for manufacturing applications [56, 57], with **edge computing nodes** (e.g., CNC machine shadows [57]) aggregating local data before cloud transmission.
   - **Hierarchical models** (e.g., factory floor → cloud) may exist, though not explicitly detailed.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - Not quantified, but **low-latency** requirements are critical for real-time manufacturing applications [56]. **Time-sensitive networking (TSN)** or **deterministic protocols** may be assumed for critical paths.

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **Redundant data paths** (e.g., failover mechanisms) are implied for manufacturing systems [56] to ensure continuous operation.
   - **Data replication** or **checkpointing** may be used for recovery.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Encryption** (e.g., TLS for MQTT) and **authentication** (e.g., OAuth, API keys) are likely enforced, though not explicitly named in the documents.
   - **Isolation mechanisms** (e.g., network segmentation) may separate critical DT components from public IoT platforms.

**Note**: Specific details (e.g., exact bandwidth thresholds, latency SLA, or redundancy protocols) are **Not in Document**.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **deployment infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems, as inferred from the documents, includes:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Cloud-based deployment** is implied for scalability (e.g., ThingsBoard [53] or open-source IoT platforms) and centralized data aggregation.
   - **Edge deployment** is referenced for **CNC machine shadows** [57], where local processing reduces cloud dependency.
   - **On-premise deployment** may exist for sensitive manufacturing data, though not explicitly mentioned.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - **Computational**: Not quantified, but **resource-intensive tasks** (e.g., simulation, AI-driven analytics) may require **high-performance computing (HPC)** or **GPU acceleration** for real-time processing.
   - **Storage**: **Time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) or **NoSQL solutions** (e.g., MongoDB) are plausible for storing contextual data traces [32].

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Horizontal scaling** (e.g., cloud auto-scaling) is implied for handling **large-scale manufacturing data** [56].
   - **Modular design** (e.g., microservices) may enable incremental DT expansion.

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Hybrid models** (cloud + edge) are referenced for CNC machines [57], balancing latency and cost.
   - **Containerization** (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) may facilitate portable DT deployments, though not mentioned.

5. **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
   - **Redundancy** (e.g., multi-region cloud deployment) and **disaster recovery** (e.g., backup replication) are implied for manufacturing criticality [56].
   - **Service-level agreements (SLAs)** for uptime (e.g., 99.9% availability) may apply, though specifics are **Not in Document**.

**Note**: Exact resource quotas (e.g., CPU/GPU cores, storage tiers) or deployment frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes vs. serverless) are **Not in Document**.
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system supports **horizontal integration** with external systems through **heterogeneous data exchange mechanisms** and **interoperability standards** implied by the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**. While no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs, MQTT) are mentioned, the model describes **data traces** as the primary medium for integration, which are **aggregated and abstracted** for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing analytics, predictive maintenance). The **Processing component** consumes input data traces from external sources (e.g., IoT sensors, enterprise systems) and produces output traces that can be **shared with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems**. The **concretization of the reference model** allows for **custom integration patterns**, such as:
   1. **Data ingestion from external systems** (e.g., SCADA, ERP) via **standardized data formats** (e.g., JSON, XML).
   2. **Interoperability with other Digital Twins** through **shared data traces** and **traceability links**, ensuring consistency across distributed instances.
   3. **Dependencies on external systems** are managed via **purpose-driven design**, where the Digital Twin system is tailored to the **specific requirements** of the external stakeholders (e.g., manufacturers, operators).
   The model does not specify **real-time integration constraints** or **security protocols**, but the **automated one-way data flow** implies reliance on **standardized communication interfaces** to facilitate seamless data exchange. The **traceability framework** further ensures that integrated data traces can be **audited and validated** across the Digital Twin system."
]

instance data_ownership_and_privacy : DTDFVocab:DataOwnershipPrivacy [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents adheres to **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** standards (per [46], [47], [50]), which mandate explicit **data ownership and privacy governance** through structured metadata and access control mechanisms. The AAS framework defines **Submodels** (e.g., *Identification*, *GlobalAssetId*, *AdministrativeInformation*) to encode **data provenance, ownership rights, and compliance requirements** (e.g., GDPR, industry-specific regulations). For **privacy protection**, the AAS specifies **semantic annotations** (e.g., *Confidentiality*, *AccessControlList*) tied to data traces, enabling granular consent management via **role-based access control (RBAC)** or **attribute-based encryption (ABE)**. **Data anonymization** is supported through **pseudonymization techniques** in simulation models (e.g., [45]’s friction modeling for robot dynamics), where raw sensor data (e.g., joint angles, torque traces) is transformed into **aggregated or perturbed representations** while preserving traceability. **Data sharing agreements** are formalized via **AAS Submodels** (e.g., *ContractualCompliance*) linking to external standards like **Industrie 4.0’s RAMI 4.0** ([49], [50]), which define **interoperability layers** (e.g., *Communication*, *Integration*) for secure data exchange. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** ([41], [42]) emphasizes **conformance relations** to ensure compliance with these governance policies during model concretization, where **data ownership metadata** is embedded in **Class Diagrams (CDs)** or **ontology mappings** to enforce traceability and auditability."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "Security and safety in the described Digital Twin system are addressed through a **multi-layered architecture** integrating **cybersecurity protocols**, **fail-safe mechanisms**, and **risk assessment frameworks**, primarily aligned with **RAMI 4.0** ([49], [50]) and **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** standards ([46], [47]). **Cybersecurity protocols** include **MQTT-based communication** (e.g., [52]’s Mosquitto broker) for **low-latency, authenticated data streams** between physical assets (e.g., MABI robotic arms [44]) and digital shadows, with **TLS 1.2+ encryption** for sensitive payloads. **Access control** is enforced via **AAS Submodels** (e.g., *AccessControl*) mapping **IAM roles** (e.g., *Operator*, *Engineer*) to **permission policies** (e.g., *read/write/execute*). **Threat mitigation** leverages **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** integrated with **ThingsBoard** ([53], an open-source IoT platform) for real-time anomaly detection in **data traces** (e.g., sensor drift, command injection). **Fail-safe mechanisms** are embedded in **simulation models** (e.g., [51]’s Functional Mockup Interface, FMI) to enforce **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) validation**, where **safety-critical behaviors** (e.g., emergency stops) are pre-validated via **model-driven engineering (MDE)** ([43]). **Risk assessment** follows **RAMI 4.0’s asset lifecycle phases** ([49]), where **safety-critical components** (e.g., robotic joints in [45]) are annotated with **ISO 26262-compliant** risk levels in AAS Submodels. **Digital Shadow traceability** ([42]) ensures **end-to-end auditability** of security events, linking **data traces** to their **processing components** (e.g., simulation engines) via **conformance relations** ([41]) to detect tampering or unauthorized modifications. Additionally, **safety-critical system considerations** are addressed through **FMI-compliant** simulation models ([51]) that enforce **real-time constraints** (e.g., <10ms latency for robotic control loops) and **hardware redundancy checks** in digital twins."
]
